efforts_to_obtain_counsel.other_material_if_any: Attached as Exhibit A are copies of five email exchanges and three written declination letters from law firms I contacted between August and November 2025, including: (1) North Sound Legal Aid; (2) Puget Civil Rights Center; (3) Larson & Patel, PLLC; (4) Greenfield Employment Law Group; and (5) Harborview Legal Clinic. These materials document my attempts to secure representation on a contingency or pro bono basis and the reasons given for declining my case, primarily limited capacity and perceived complexity of federal litigation.
efforts_to_obtain_counsel.efforts_to_obtain_counsel_line_1: Since August 2025, I have contacted at least eight different attorneys or legal organizations in the Seattle area, including two legal aid organizations, three private employment law firms, one civil rights nonprofit, and two solo practitioners, to ask if they would represent me in this employment discrimination and retaliation case on a contingency or reduced-fee basis.
efforts_to_obtain_counsel.efforts_to_obtain_counsel_line_2: North Sound Legal Aid and Harborview Legal Clinic both informed me that due to staffing shortages and existing caseloads, they were not able to accept new federal civil cases at this time; North Sound Legal Aid specifically stated by email on September 3, 2025, that their waitlist for employment cases was closed for at least six months.
efforts_to_obtain_counsel.efforts_to_obtain_counsel_line_3: The private firms I contacted—Greenfield Employment Law Group, Larson & Patel, PLLC, and Westlake Workplace Justice—each reviewed a summary of my case and some of my documents but ultimately declined representation, explaining that although they believed I might have viable claims, the anticipated litigation costs and the defendant’s likely aggressive defense strategy made the case financially infeasible for them on a contingency basis given my inability to pay a retainer.
efforts_to_obtain_counsel.efforts_to_obtain_counsel_line_4: Two solo practitioners, Attorney Melissa Cho and Attorney Daniel R. Higgins, advised me in brief phone consultations that while they sometimes accept federal employment matters, they would require an advance retainer of between $4,000 and $7,500, which I cannot afford due to my current unemployment, limited savings of under $1,000, and ongoing medical expenses related to the anxiety and depression I developed after losing my job.
efforts_to_obtain_counsel.efforts_to_obtain_counsel_line_5: In addition to these efforts, I have contacted the county bar association’s lawyer referral service twice, in September and October 2025, and was told that no attorneys in their referral panel were available to take my case on a contingency or pro bono basis; I have exhausted the realistic options I can identify, and without appointed counsel I do not believe I can adequately handle discovery, motion practice, and potential trial on my own given the complexity of federal rules and my limited legal knowledge.
